schools.summary: The finished creation: 'thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them' — the work of creation, the heavens and earth and all that fills them, completed ('all the host of them' embracing the sun, moon, and stars, the host of heaven, and the creatures of the earth, the whole creation in all its fullness finished); the creation 'finished' brought to its completion and perfection (nothing lacking or unfinished, God's six-day work resulting in a complete, ordered, and furnished world); 'all the host of them' the numerous and varied works of creation, marshaled like an army in their order and array (the heavens with their host, the stars, the earth with its creatures, the orderly complete furnishing of the world, all things in their ranks and stations) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them
the completion of the heavens and earth and all their host
the whole creation completed and furnished
the orderly fullness of the heavens and earth

schools.summary: God's rest on the seventh day: 'and on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made' — God bringing his creative work to its close (the work finished by the seventh day, the sense being that by the seventh day God had completed it, and on it he ceased, the six days of creating ending and the seventh the day of completion and rest); 'he rested (shabath — ceased) on the seventh day from all his work' (God's resting not from weariness, the eternal God not tiring, Isaiah 40:28, but the ceasing from the work of creation, he stopped creating new things, the work being complete, the rest the cessation of the creative labor); 'from all his work which he had made' (he ceased from the work of the six days, not that he ceased to uphold and govern, his providence continuing, John 5:17, but that the work of original creation was finished, God's rest setting the pattern for man's sabbath rest) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work
God ending his work and resting on the seventh day
the ceasing from creative labor, not from weariness
God's rest the pattern of the sabbath

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill: 'God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it (set it apart as holy)' — God blessing and consecrating the seventh day, distinguishing it from the others, the day hallowed because God rested on it, sanctified 'because that in it he had rested from all his work' (God's own rest the ground and pattern of the sabbath, the day commemorating the completion of creation, man to rest as God rested); the Sabbath instituted and sanctified at creation, from the beginning, for all mankind — the seventh-day rest set apart by God at the world's foundation, a creation ordinance binding on the whole human race, not merely on Israel, established before the fall and before the law.

Clarke notes the alternative view: that the religious observance of the Sabbath did not begin until the days of Moses (Sinai), and that the blessing and sanctifying here are recorded proleptically (anticipating the later institution given to Israel, the formal sabbath-law belonging to the Mosaic covenant, the seventh-day observance dated by this view from Sinai rather than creation). The crux: the Sabbath a creation ordinance for all mankind, or a Mosaic institution for Israel, the creation-blessing recorded by way of anticipation.

debates.question: When was the Sabbath instituted — at creation for all mankind, or at Sinai for Israel?

sides.position: At creation, for all mankind — a creation ordinance, the seventh day sanctified from the beginning
At Sinai, for Israel — the formal sabbath-observance beginning with Moses, the creation-blessing recorded proleptically

themes: God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because that in it he had rested from all his work
the blessing and sanctifying of the seventh day
the crux of the Sabbath's institution (creation or Sinai)
God's rest the ground and pattern of the sabbath

schools.summary: The generations-heading and the divine name: 'these are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens' — the formula 'these are the generations (toledoth)' (recurring through Genesis) introducing a new section, here heading the account that follows (the fuller account of man and the garden, 2:4-25, the 'generations' denoting the history, origin, and developments of the heavens and earth); the verse looking back to the creation (ch. 1) and forward to the account of man ('in the day' used broadly for the time of creation, the second account, 2:4ff., not contradicting the first but focusing on man and his garden, complementing the cosmic account of ch. 1); 'the LORD God' (Yahweh Elohim) the compound name appearing for the first time, joining the covenant name 'LORD' (Yahweh) with 'God' (Elohim) — the same God who created the covenant LORD who deals with man, the name marking God as both Creator and the personal, covenant God (some critics seeing different sources in the change of names, but the conservative reading taking the names as marking the different aspects of the one God's work) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created
in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens
the heading of the generations and the divine name LORD God
God both Creator (Elohim) and covenant Lord (Yahweh)

schools.summary: The earth before cultivation: 'and every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew: for the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man to till the ground' — the account describing the state of the earth before the cultivated plants grew (the 'plant of the field' and 'herb of the field' the cultivated vegetation, the plants of agriculture, which awaited rain and human tillage, the wild vegetation of ch. 1 already made but the cultivated growth awaiting the conditions of v.5-6); 'the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth' (no rain yet, the earth watered by a mist, v.6, the ordinary means of cultivation not yet operating); 'there was not a man to till the ground' (no man to cultivate the soil, the cultivated plants awaiting both rain and the labor of man, the verse preparing for the forming of man, v.7, to till the garden, the earth awaiting its keeper) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew
the LORD God had not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man to till the ground
the earth before cultivation, without rain or tiller
the earth awaiting both rain and the labor of man

schools.summary: The mist watering the ground: 'but there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground' — before the rain, a mist (or vapor) rising from the earth and watering the whole surface of the ground (the mist supplying the moisture needed for the vegetation, God providing for the earth's watering by this means before the ordinary rains began); 'there went up a mist from the earth' (the rising vapor the means God used to water the ground in the original state, whether a daily mist or the primeval watering, it sufficed to nourish the plants, the divine provision for the earth's moisture); 'watered the whole face of the ground' (the whole surface moistened and made fruitful, God's provision sustaining the vegetation before rain, the earth prepared and watered for life and growth, the divine care for the creation's nourishment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground
the mist watering the ground
God's provision moistening the earth before the rain
the earth prepared and nourished for life

schools.summary: The forming of man: 'and the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul' — God shaping man's body from the dust ('formed,' yatsar, as a potter shapes clay, the word picturing the divine artistry, fashioning man's frame from the ground, adamah, whence 'Adam,' man's body of the earth, earthy, made of the dust); 'breathed into his nostrils the breath of life' (God imparting to the formed body the breath of life, the living, rational soul, the soul not of the dust but the immediate gift of God's inbreathing, man's higher nature, the spirit, from God, the breath of life making the dust-formed body a living person); 'man became a living soul (nephesh chayyah)' (by the union of the dust-formed body and the divine breath, man a living being, a compound of earthly body and God-given life, the dignity of man — body from the dust and soul from God's breath — the creature uniquely formed and inspired by God) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul
the forming of man from dust and the breath of life
man's body of the earth and his soul the gift of God's inbreathing
the creature uniquely formed and inspired by God

schools.summary: The garden of Eden: 'and the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there he put the man whom he had formed' — God planting a garden (a park, paradise) in the region of Eden, to the east (Eden, 'delight,' the place, the garden a special enclosure within it, God himself preparing this delightful, fruitful garden as man's first dwelling); 'a garden eastward in Eden' (Eden a real region, located by the rivers, vv.10-14, to the east, the garden a place of beauty, fruitfulness, and delight, planted by God for man, the paradise prepared for the first man); 'there he put the man whom he had formed' (God placing man, formed of the dust, v.7, in this prepared, delightful dwelling, the garden man's home, the place of his innocence, fellowship with God, and appointed work, v.15, God's care providing man a fit and happy habitation) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Gill.

themes: the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there he put the man whom he had formed
the planting of the garden of Eden and the placing of man
the delightful, fruitful paradise prepared as man's first dwelling
man's home and the place of his fellowship with God

schools.summary: The trees of the garden: 'and out of the ground made the LORD God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil' — God making the garden's trees both beautiful (pleasant to the sight) and fruitful (good for food), the garden providing for man's delight and sustenance, the trees combining beauty and use, the bounty of God; 'the tree of life... in the midst of the garden' (whether a sacramental sign and pledge of continued life, a sign and seal of the life God gave to confirm man in life, or a tree whose fruit sustained life, the tree of life a pledge and means of life, pointing to the life that is in God); 'the tree of knowledge of good and evil' (the tree by which man's obedience would be tested, v.17, so called because by it man would come to know good and evil experimentally, the tree of the probation, the test of man's loyalty to God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil
the trees of the garden, the tree of life, and the tree of knowledge
the garden of delight and sustenance with its two special trees
the pledge of life and the test of obedience

schools.summary: The river of Eden: 'and a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from thence it was parted, and became into four heads' — a river flowing from Eden, watering the garden (the river supplying the garden's abundant moisture and fruitfulness, God providing the garden with a copious, living stream); 'from thence it was parted, and became into four heads' (the river dividing into four streams or branches, heads, named in vv.11-14 — Pison, Gihon, Hiddekel, Euphrates — the geography locating Eden in a real region watered by these rivers, the precise identification much debated but the rivers marking Eden as an actual place); the river's abundance and division marking the richness and well-watered fertility of Eden (the garden lacking nothing for its fruitfulness, God's provision generous and complete, the geography rooting the paradise in a real, identifiable land) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from thence it was parted, and became into four heads
the river of Eden parting into four heads
the abundant stream watering paradise
Eden located in a real, watered region

schools.summary: The first river Pison: 'the name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold' — the first of the four rivers named Pison, which winds about (compasses) the land of Havilah, a region noted for gold (the geography naming the rivers and lands, locating Eden in an actual setting); Pison flowing around the land of Havilah (the precise identification of Pison and Havilah uncertain, variously located, but the river and land named as real places, the geography marking Eden's region); 'where there is gold' (the land of Havilah noted for its gold and other precious things, v.12, the mention of gold and precious stones marking the richness of the lands about Eden, the region one of natural wealth and beauty) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold
the first river Pison and the land of Havilah
the rivers and lands named as real places
the richness of Eden's region

schools.summary: The precious things of Havilah: 'and the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone' — the land of Havilah (v.11) rich in good gold, and also in bdellium (an aromatic gum or precious substance) and the onyx stone (a precious gem), the region abounding in natural wealth and precious things (the mention marking the richness and beauty of the lands about Eden); 'the gold of that land is good' (the gold of Havilah of high quality, fine and pure, the land's wealth noted, the goodness of its gold marking its richness); 'there is bdellium and the onyx stone' (bdellium, variously identified as a fragrant gum or a precious stone/pearl, and the onyx, a precious gem, the land yielding these precious things, the abundance of gold, gum, and gems marking the richness of the region, Eden's setting one of natural treasure and beauty) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the onyx stone
the precious things of Havilah
the land abounding in gold, gum, and gems
the natural wealth and beauty of Eden's region

schools.summary: The second river Gihon: 'and the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia (Cush)' — the second of the four rivers named Gihon, which winds about the land of Cush (rendered 'Ethiopia'), the geography continuing to name the rivers and lands of Eden's region; Gihon flowing around the land of Cush (the identification of Gihon and Cush debated, Cush variously located in Africa or the East, but the river and land named as real places, marking Eden's setting); the naming of the four rivers (Pison, Gihon, Hiddekel, Euphrates) and their lands locating Eden in an actual, identifiable region (though the precise placement uncertain, the flood having altered the geography, the rivers marking Eden as a real place, not a myth, the historical, geographical setting of the garden) — Gill, Ellicott, JFB.

themes: the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia
the second river Gihon and the land of Cush
the rivers naming Eden's region
the garden a real, located place

schools.summary: The third and fourth rivers: 'and the name of the third river is Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is Euphrates' — the third river Hiddekel (commonly identified with the Tigris), flowing east of Assyria, and the fourth the Euphrates (the two best-known rivers, the Tigris and Euphrates, anchoring the geography of Eden's region); 'Hiddekel: that is it which goeth toward the east of Assyria' (Hiddekel the Tigris, flowing eastward of Assyria, the well-known river locating Eden's region in the area of Mesopotamia, the land of the Tigris and Euphrates); 'the fourth river is Euphrates' (the Euphrates, the great river, needing no further description, being well known, the naming of the Tigris and Euphrates placing Eden's region in or near Mesopotamia, the geography rooting paradise in the historic land of the great rivers) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: the name of the third river is Hiddekel... And the fourth river is Euphrates
the third and fourth rivers, Hiddekel and Euphrates
the Tigris and Euphrates anchoring Eden's region
paradise in the historic land of the great rivers

schools.summary: Man set to dress and keep the garden: 'and the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it' — God placing man (formed of dust, v.7) in the garden, the man settled in his appointed dwelling (the verse resuming and expanding v.8, focusing now on man's work and command); 'to dress it (till, cultivate) and to keep it (guard, tend)' (man, even in innocence, given work: to cultivate and care for the garden, labor not a result of the fall but a part of man's original, happy estate, idleness not the condition of paradise, man to tend and keep God's garden); man's appointed dressing and keeping of the garden showing that honest labor is good and honorable (God setting man to useful work in the place of his innocence, the cultivation of the garden a happy, fitting employment, not toil or curse, the toil coming with the fall, 3:17-19) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it
man placed in the garden to dress and keep it
labor part of man's original, happy estate, not a result of the fall
the dignity of honest labor in paradise

schools.summary: The gracious permission: 'and the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat' — God giving man a command, the test of his obedience (the command, vv.16-17, the first law given to man, the trial of his loyalty, God as man's Lord and benefactor laying his authority on the man he made); 'of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat' (God's first word to man one of generous permission, he may eat freely of every tree, save one, v.17, the bounty and liberality of God toward man, the prohibition a single, small restraint amid abundant liberty, God's goodness giving much and forbidding little); the command, with its generous permission and single prohibition, the test of man's obedience and gratitude (God's liberality leaving no excuse for transgression, the one restraint the trial of man's loyalty to his Maker, man's happy estate depending on his obedience) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Gill.

themes: the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat
God's gracious permission to eat of every tree
the bounty and liberality of God, the prohibition a small restraint
the command the test of man's obedience

schools.summary: The prohibition and threatened death: 'but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die' — God forbidding man to eat of the one tree (v.9), the single prohibition the test of man's obedience (the tree not poisonous in itself but forbidden by God's command, the trial lying in obedience to God's word, the one restraint amid all the liberty, v.16, the test of man's submission to his Maker, obedience to God's word the condition of man's happy estate); 'in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die (dying thou shalt die)' (the penalty of disobedience death, the death threatened embracing spiritual death — separation from God, beginning at once — physical death — the dissolution of body and soul, now made man's lot — and eternal death — the second death, apart from grace; 'surely die,' the doubled verb, marking the certainty, in the day of eating man becoming subject to death) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die
the prohibition and the threatened death
the single forbidden tree the test of obedience
death (spiritual, physical, and eternal) the penalty of transgression

schools.summary: The helper for man: 'and the LORD God said, It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him an help meet for him' — God declaring man's solitary state not good (man, made for society and companionship, incomplete alone, the only thing in the creation pronounced 'not good' man's solitude, God purposing to remedy it, man needing a companion suited to him); man's being alone not good (he needed a companion and helper, God, who knows man's nature, providing for his need, marriage and human society God's gracious provision, man made for fellowship); 'I will make him an help meet (a help suitable, corresponding) for him' (God purposing to make a helper fit and corresponding to man, the woman made to be his suitable partner, helper, and companion, 'meet for him' — suitable, answering to him — marking her fitness as his counterpart, the divine institution of the woman as man's helper and the foundation of marriage) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him an help meet for him
God's purpose to make a helper for man
man made for companionship, his solitude not good
the woman God's provision as man's fit helper, the foundation of marriage

schools.summary: The animals named by Adam: 'and out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them: and whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof' — the animals, made of the ground (as their bodies, ch. 1), here brought before man (the verse not asserting a new creation but recalling the creatures' origin and their being brought to Adam, the beasts and birds presented to man); God 'brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them' (God presenting the creatures to Adam to name them, the naming showing Adam's authority and dominion over the creatures, 1:28, and his knowledge and wisdom in discerning their natures, man as lord of the creatures giving them their names); 'whatsoever Adam called every living creature, that was the name thereof' (Adam's names standing, his naming reflecting the natures of the creatures, his original wisdom and insight, the naming also showing that among all the creatures none was a fit helper for man, v.20, preparing for the making of the woman) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them
the animals brought to Adam to be named
Adam's dominion and original wisdom in naming the creatures
none among the creatures a fit helper for man

schools.summary: The naming and the unmet need: 'and Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him' — Adam naming all the creatures (v.19), but among them all there being found no helper suitable for him (the naming displaying his dominion but also making evident his solitude, none of the animals able to be his companion and counterpart); Adam naming all the classes of creatures (cattle, fowl, beasts, the naming comprehensive, man exercising his dominion over all the living creatures); 'but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him' (among all the creatures none a fit helper, companion, or counterpart for man, the beasts, though under his dominion, unable to supply his need for fellowship, the lack preparing for and underscoring the gift of the woman, vv.21-22, man needing one of his own nature) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not found an help meet for him
Adam naming the creatures and finding no fit helper
man's dominion over the creatures and his unmet need for a companion
the lack preparing for the gift of the woman

schools.summary: The deep sleep and the rib: 'and the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof' — God putting Adam into a deep, supernatural sleep (tardemah), that he might feel no pain and witness nothing of the operation (the deep sleep God's gracious provision while he forms the woman from him, man's part passive, the work wholly God's); God taking a rib (or a part of Adam's side) and closing the flesh (the woman made from man's own body, from his side, the taking of the rib — not from the head to rule him, nor the foot to be trampled, but the side to be his equal and near his heart, as the expositors observe — marking the woman's intimate union with man); the woman, made from man's rib, bone of his bone (v.23), the manner of her making teaching the closeness, union, and mutual love of husband and wife (taken from his side, to be his companion and equal, dear to him as his own flesh) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof
the deep sleep and the taking of the rib
the woman formed from man's own side
the manner of her making teaching the union and equality of husband and wife

schools.summary: The making of the woman: 'and the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a woman, and brought her unto the man' — God forming (the word banah means 'built') the woman from the rib taken from man (the woman God's own handiwork, built from man's side, God then bringing her to the man, presenting her as his helper and bride); 'made (built) he a woman' (the word marking the careful, skillful forming of the woman, she fashioned by God from man's body, the woman no afterthought but God's deliberate, gracious provision, built to be man's counterpart); 'brought her unto the man' (God himself bringing the woman to Adam, presenting her as his helper and wife, God as it were the author and witness of the first marriage, the woman, God's gift, given to the man, the divine institution of marriage, God joining the man and woman) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a woman, and brought her unto the man
the making of the woman and her presentation to man
the woman God's own handiwork, built from man's side
God himself the author of the first marriage

schools.summary: Adam's recognition of the woman: 'and Adam said, This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man' — Adam, awaking, recognizing the woman as part of himself, taken from his own body ('now at last,' this is now, marking his glad recognition, after the creatures had yielded no fit helper, v.20, the woman of his own nature, bone and flesh of his); 'bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh' (the woman of one nature and substance with man, the closest union and kinship, husband and wife one flesh, v.24, the woman man's equal and intimate companion, not of a different or inferior kind); 'she shall be called Woman (ishshah), because she was taken out of Man (ish)' (Adam naming her 'Woman,' corresponding to 'Man,' the name marking her origin from man and her kinship with him, the wordplay ish/ishshah expressing their unity and correspondence, the woman named by the man as one of his own) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man
Adam's recognition of the woman as his own flesh
the woman of one nature with man, the closest union
the woman named by the man as one of his own

schools.summary: The institution of marriage: 'therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh' — the verse drawing from the making of the woman (vv.21-23) the ordinance of marriage (a man shall leave his parents and cleave — be joined, united — to his wife, marriage the closest of human bonds, taking precedence even over the tie to father and mother, the man and wife forming a new union); 'shall cleave unto his wife' (the marriage bond permanent, faithful, and exclusive, the husband cleaving to his wife in a lasting union, marriage a lifelong, faithful cleaving, not to be broken, cf. Matthew 19:5-6, where Christ cites this verse for the permanence of marriage); 'they shall be one flesh' (the man and wife becoming one flesh, a single union, the deepest bodily, personal, and spiritual union, the two becoming one, the foundation of marriage as instituted by God at creation, before the fall, Christ and Paul building on this, Matthew 19; Ephesians 5:31, for the doctrine of marriage) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh
the institution of marriage and one flesh
the closest, permanent, faithful union of husband and wife
marriage instituted by God at creation, the two made one

schools.summary: The innocence of the pair: 'and they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed' — Adam and Eve naked, without clothing (in their original innocence, having no need of covering, the unfallen pair unclothed yet undisturbed); 'and were not ashamed' (their nakedness bringing no shame, for there was as yet no sin, no lust, no consciousness of evil, their innocence perfect, shame entering only with sin, 3:7, when their eyes were opened, the absence of shame marking the purity and integrity of the unfallen state); the pair's nakedness without shame marking their original righteousness and innocence (after the fall, 3:7, they knew shame and sought to cover themselves, the verse closing the account of the creation in the happy, innocent state of man and wife before sin entered, the loss of this innocence the tragedy of the fall, ch. 3) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed
the innocence of the unashamed pair
the original righteousness and innocence of the unfallen state
the happy state of man before the fall
